metadata.title: Checking Out Charitable Groups
metadata.subtitle: Evaluating Nonprofits to Maximize Your Impact

content.title: Checking Out Charitable Groups
content.subtitle: Evaluating Nonprofits to Maximize Your Impact

content.label: The Challenge
content.question:
How can you ensure your charitable donations
have the greatest positive impact and don't end up
supporting ineffective or fraudulent organizations?

objectives.description: Recognize the importance of researching charitable organizations before donating money, time, or resources.
Assess the transparency and financial health of nonprofit organizations using available tools and resources.
Use the PACED decision-making model to compare and select charitable organizations to support.
Recognize red flags and warning signs of ineffective or potentially fraudulent charitable organizations.

terms.definition: A tax-exempt organization that uses surplus revenue to achieve its mission rather than distributing profits to owners or shareholders.
terms.example: Charities, foundations, educational institutions, religious organizations

terms.definition: IRS designation (typically 501(c)(3)) that allows organizations to receive tax-deductible donations and avoid paying federal income tax.
terms.example: Donations to 501(c)(3) organizations can be deducted from your taxable income

terms.definition: Annual information return that nonprofits must file with the IRS, publicly available and showing financial details and programs.
terms.example: Shows revenue, expenses, executive compensation, and program descriptions

terms.definition: The percentage of total expenses that go directly to programs and services rather than administrative costs or fundraising.
terms.example: If a charity spends $80 on programs for every $100 in expenses, the program expense ratio is 80%

terms.definition: The cost required to raise each dollar of donations, measuring how effectively an organization acquires funding.
terms.example: If it costs $0.20 to raise $1.00, the fundraising efficiency is strong

terms.definition: The degree to which a nonprofit openly shares financial information, program outcomes, and governance details with the public.
terms.example: Publishing annual reports, Form 990s, and impact measurements on their website

leftPanel.title: Not All Charities Are Equal
paragraphs: Charitable giving is powerful, but not all organizations use donations with the same efficiency or effectiveness.
Some may allocate large portions of funds to administrative costs rather than direct programs.
Others might not be legitimate at all—charity scams cost Americans hundreds of millions of dollars each year.

highlightBox.text: By developing research skills, you become a more informed and impactful donor.

layoutData.title: Independent Resources for Research
paragraphs: Several organizations provide free tools to help you research and compare charitable groups:
bulletPoints: 🌟 Charity Navigator: Star ratings, financial health metrics, 9,000+ charities evaluated
📋 GuideStar: Access to Form 990 tax returns, transparency ratings, 1.8M+ organizations
✓ BBB Wise Giving Alliance: Evaluates against 20 accountability standards
📊 CharityWatch: Grades on financial efficiency, highlights concerns like excessive reserves
layoutData.keyPoint: 💡 Use multiple sources—each tool evaluates charities using different criteria, giving you a more complete picture.

leftPanel.title: Key Metrics to Review
paragraphs: Program Expense Ratio: What % of funds goes directly to programs vs. overhead? (70%+ is generally good)
Fundraising Efficiency: How much does it cost to raise $1? (Under $0.25 is efficient)
Working Capital: Does the organization have enough resources to sustain operations?
Executive Compensation: Are leadership salaries reasonable for the organization's size?

highlightBox.text: Remember: Some overhead is necessary for effective operations. The key is finding the right balance.

layoutData.title: A Structured Approach for Comparing Charities
paragraphs: When choosing between multiple charitable organizations, use the PACED model to make an informed decision:
bulletPoints: Problem: Define what cause you want to support
Alternatives: Identify 2-3 organizations working on that cause
Criteria: Determine what matters most to you (efficiency, transparency, local vs. global, etc.)
Evaluate: Research each organization using charity evaluation tools
Decide: Choose the organization that best meets your criteria
layoutData.keyPoint: 💡 Your criteria might include: program effectiveness, financial health, local vs. global impact, transparency, alignment with values.

leftPanel.title: Watch Out For These Red Flags
paragraphs: 🚩 Pressure for immediate donations: Legitimate charities give you time to research
🚩 Vague or emotional appeals: Look for specific program details and outcomes
🚩 Refusal to provide documentation: Legitimate charities share financial statements
🚩 Name confusion: Scammers use names similar to well-known charities
🚩 Cash-only donations: Legitimate charities accept checks and credit cards with receipts

stats.label: Scam Tactic: Pressure for Immediate Donations

leftPanel.title: How to Confirm Legitimacy
paragraphs: Before donating, always verify that an organization is a legitimate 501(c)(3) nonprofit:
1. IRS Tax Exempt Organization Search: Use the IRS online database to confirm tax-exempt status
2. Request EIN (Employer Identification Number): Legitimate nonprofits will provide this
3. Check State Registration: Most states require charities to register before soliciting donations
4. Review Form 990: Available on GuideStar and the organization's website

highlightBox.text: If an organization refuses to provide their EIN or tax documentation, that's a major red flag.

leftPanel.title: Does the Charity Measure Its Impact?
paragraphs: Beyond financial metrics, effective charities track and report their actual impact:
• How many people were served?
• What specific outcomes were achieved?
• How do results compare to goals?
• What evidence supports their effectiveness?

highlightBox.text: The best charities publish annual impact reports with specific metrics and stories demonstrating real results.

scenario.name: Maria's PACED Analysis
paragraphs: Maria has $500 to donate for environmental conservation. She wants to make sure her money has real impact, so she researched two organizations using evaluation tools:
EcoAction: 82% to programs, local focus in her state, 15 years operating, strong financial health with steady growth, limited published outcome data
Green Planet Alliance: 75% to programs, global policy focus on climate legislation, 8 years operating, detailed impact reports with specific metrics, experiencing rapid growth

Using the PACED model, Maria decided EcoAction better aligned with her values—she prefers supporting local environmental efforts with lower overhead and a proven track record, even though they have less detailed reporting.

outcomes.label: Confidence Level
outcomes.detail: Informed decision based on her criteria

scenario.name: John Identifies a Scam
paragraphs: John received a phone call from "Children's Cancer Relief Fund" pressuring him for an immediate credit card donation. The caller mentioned a "matching gift deadline" expiring in 30 minutes and used high-pressure emotional appeals.
Instead of donating immediately, John:
1. Asked for the organization's website and EIN (they became evasive)
2. Searched the organization name on Charity Navigator (not found)
3. Checked the IRS Tax Exempt Organization Search (not registered)
4. Reported the call to the Federal Trade Commission
John's research skills protected him from a $250 scam. He later donated that amount to a verified children's cancer charity with a 4-star Charity Navigator rating.

content.question:
Imagine you received a $1,000 inheritance that must be donated to charity.

What specific criteria would you use to evaluate organizations?
How would you balance admin costs vs. program effectiveness vs. alignment with your values?

Have you or your family ever researched a charity before donating?

What tools or methods did you use?
After learning about charity evaluation, what would you do differently next time?

questions.question: What are three free tools you can use to research charitable organizations before donating?
Explain what the Program Expense Ratio measures and why it matters to donors.
List three red flags that might indicate a charity is ineffective or fraudulent.
How does the PACED model help you make better charitable giving decisions?

takeaways.title: Research Before Giving
takeaways.description: Not all charities are equally effective—researching ensures your donations have real impact

takeaways.title: Use Evaluation Tools
takeaways.description: Charity Navigator, GuideStar, and BBB Wise Giving provide free research resources

takeaways.title: Watch for Red Flags
takeaways.description: Pressure tactics, lack of transparency, and vague missions are warning signs

takeaways.title: Apply PACED Model
takeaways.description: Use a structured decision process to compare organizations against YOUR criteria

main.title: Charity Research Activity
main.description: Practice researching a real charitable organization to ensure your future donations have maximum positive impact. Choose a charity you're familiar with or interested in supporting, then use evaluation tools to gather information.

steps: Look up their Charity Navigator rating
Decide: Would you support this charity?
